title: Cross Cultural Translations
wikitext:
[]: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Archaea » Sun Jun 20, 2010 12:51 am before I start- there are currently no spoilers- but that is subject to change, and in all likelihood will. Hopefully it will end up full of em'! -Admittedly this is more of a question about overall comprehension and cross cultural translation, but I feel (as I hope you will) that it belongs here in the in-depth discussion section. I think it might be best to explain my inquiry through an example: Take the word “Angel” for instance. What comes to mind after reading that word? Evangelion angels (of course, but what else?), Religion: Holy entities perhaps (winged beings with bright auras, The Christian God, or maybe even Lucifer). Going further than religion and into a tertiary meaning of the world -Angel can define- a beautiful woman, benevolent person, and a mother. Even further, the word angel can be used to describe any manner of pleasant (beautiful), and enchanting creatures [e.g. Fish]. As an afterthought, I probably should have used a less context sensitive and more Westernized example. -Phonetically lets look at the word Fat.- it can mean something that is “cool”, or be used to paint the picture of an overweight person. The word “cool” can be used to describe temperature, or mean that something is excellent. The word cold can then be used to describe someone’s attitude or mindset as well as the temperature. I digress… Getting to the point, words (and phrases) can hold very different meanings and refer to a myriad of historical and cultural events, given the context in which they are used. My question pertains mainly to the subtitled translation of the show, but can just as easily be applied to the dubbing of it. Basically this all revolves around the question of whether or not the American and Japanese cultures are similar enough, and the show translated well enough to recreate and convey the multitude of subtle messages and connotations inherent in the original Japanese voicing of NGE.- a nearly impossible question which I don’t plan to have answered here, although I am sure that it is one that this community could handle- It seems very unlikely that a 1:1 translation could have occurred (due to societal and language-restricting differences), and thus my reason for creating this thread comes out. -To get some sort of insight into the subtle messages present in the original voicing, and to inquire in the presence of all you Evangelion scholars (/sincere)- how accurately is everything translated? I know this seems like an impossible question- and it is partially directed at those with an extensive knowledge of the Japanese world and Japanese language (e.g. Fluent speakers, and those who have researched and/or lived there), but I am curious what all of you think! What are some differences you have run across in the dub and sub- And more importantly (if anyone has spotted any), what are some of the- What You Would Consider Important , differences in the phrasing and wordage of the original Japanese voiced version and the English subtitled (or dubbed) version of Neon Genesis Evangelion? Every word (and phrase) can convey an infinite amount of meaning. No instance of inconsistency is to small to be mentioned! hopefully somebody will begin this shortly.. PS. I understand that many of the messages found in Evangelion relate to human nature and are somewhat universal- But I am still interested in What I have overlooked linguistically. Edit: removed an extra [/i]

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jun 20, 2010 1:59 am Archaea wrote: Words (and phrases) can hold very different meanings and refer to a myriad of historical and cultural events, given the context in which they are used.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Sun Jun 20, 2010 2:12 am Also, conveniently, "shito" sounds almost the same as "hito" (person). I also very frequently hear "hito" pronounced in a way very similar to "shito". This similarity, I think, is especially highlighted in EoE when Hyuga goes. "Pattern blue!" "No way, a shito?" "...No... It's a hito, a human!" The word "hito" is even spelled out in katakana here, which in similar contexts I have read as being used to emphasize the spelling/pronunciation. (Spelled out in the equivalent scene in NGE2, that is.)

[]:             Quote Post by Archaea » Sun Jun 20, 2010 2:27 am [Eva Yojimbo] Alright Mr. Sarcasm, I suppose I deserved that- I was originally going to include this in the original post, but fearing TL R s cut it out--- (too long didn't read) The understanding of both societal norms and cultural values is paramount to the comprehension of another “worlds” art- can anyone grant insight into some of the more important, totally different facets of the Japanese world? I have somewhat studied the cultural values of Japan in school, but only just. So, as well as looking at differences in the direct translation, how about trying to widen the topical matter to include the differences in Cultural Values and Societal Norms between the US and Japan. Or if you think it will suffice, we could simplify matters by generalizing and looking at just Western Vs. Eastern norms&values.

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Jun 20, 2010 2:31 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Well, you brought up Angels, and in Japanese they were actually called Shito which is closer to Messenger and, IIRC, related to something Biblical but not actual Angels.

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Archaea » Sun Jun 20, 2010 2:57 am Mr. Tines wrote: There the translation into English was a decision by Gainax (presumably Anno) to pull a foreign word with some different set of connotations to the TV audience -- recalling Tsurumaki's statement about the use of religious imagery as chrome -- to that perceived by too many American viewers and writers of fanfic.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Sun Jun 20, 2010 3:13 am Shinsei can mean (deep breath): divinity, inborn nature, genuine, application, request, petition, authentic, true, pure, holiness, sacredness, dignity, rebirth, new birth, nascent, new star, nova, and new system.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Sun Jun 20, 2010 3:29 am This is a question of whether to translate through the methods of alienation or domestication. Each one has its own terrible problems so pick your own poison. Mixing the two is even worse.

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Jun 20, 2010 4:00 am Archaea wrote: Shin seiki Evangelion Gekijô-ban: Shito shinsei Evangelion: Death & Rebirth (undefined)

[]:             Quote Post by Dr. Nick » Sun Jun 20, 2010 11:13 am Oz wrote: This is a question of whether to translate through the methods of alienation or domestication. Each one has its own terrible problems so pick your own poison. Mixing the two is even worse.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Sun Jun 20, 2010 4:59 pm I got told. I limited my thought process to ponder only on the effect of alienation in any given situation. If the viewer is already comfortable with the domesticated translation it's very likely to put him/her off if you used an alienation strategy suddenly. I guess I just didn't give enough thought to the matter this time.

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jun 20, 2010 5:36 pm Archaea wrote: Shin seiki Evangelion Gekijô-ban: Shito shinsei Evangelion: Death & Rebirth (undefined) Shito Shinsei has no direct translation (at least whoever wrote this had no idea), but just Shito alone translates to Apostle

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Jun 20, 2010 7:18 pm Mr. Tines wrote: There the translation into English was a decision by Gainax (presumably Anno) to pull a foreign word with some different set of connotations to the TV audience --

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by The Fourth Evangelion » Sun Jun 20, 2010 8:20 pm Archaea wrote: a beautiful woman, benevolent person, and a mother. Even further, the word angel can be used to describe any manner of pleasant (beautiful), and enchanting creatures [e.g. Fish].

[]: Re: Cross-Cultural Translations             Quote Post by Archaea » Sun Jun 20, 2010 9:02 pm yea there is definitely a lot of superfluous crap in my first post- I knew that I should have gone for shorter, more concise post. Eva Yojimbo wrote: The thing is, a full detailing of the cultural difference between Japan and the West and how that relates to the translation and audience comprehension of a work of art is a tremendously huge subject that could be fit for a 50k word thesis. I doubt you're going to find anyone here willing to offer such a comprehensive analysis. There has been a lot written about this but usually in bits and pieces and with particularly examples cited. If someone wanted to translate all of Hiroki Azuma's writings I think he's written a lot about NGE in this context. One article of his is available online (Anime or Something Like it).

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Mon Jun 21, 2010 9:53 pm Are you expecting lots of ethnic Japanese people or Japanese studies Master's (lol) to be here?

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Tue Jun 22, 2010 4:00 am chaosakita wrote: Are you expecting lots of ethnic Japanese people or Japanese studies Master's (lol) to be here?

[]:             Quote Post by Archaea » Tue Jun 22, 2010 11:32 pm chaosakita wrote: Are you expecting lots of ethnic Japanese people or Japanese studies Master's (lol) to be here?

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Jun 23, 2010 12:51 am Archaea wrote: I was just thinking more of people posting random differences that they have noticed here and there- with as many dedicated and posting fans that this forum has, I thought that there could be a great exchange of info-- relating to, well you know the rest.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Wed Jun 23, 2010 9:23 am are we discussing only Japanese -> english translation? 'cause the first thing that sprung to mind when i saw this tread was the pilots' designation. what in Japanese was the 1st/2nd/3rd/Xth "children" (and if i'm not mistaken in english it was 1st/2nd/3rd/Xth "child"), was translated as "chosen one" in the latin american translation. it's a stupid little detail, of course, since both words same the same purpose in context, but i always felt it lacked... something. "children"/"child" always seemed more meaningful to me. there was a second layer to it that "chosen" just didn't have. "chosen" seemed a lot more detached and, well, bureaucratic in a way, and had none of the irony the original had.

"children"/"child" always seemed more meaningful to me. there was a second layer to it that "chosen" just didn't have. "chosen" seemed a lot more detached and, well, bureaucratic in a way, and had none of the irony the original had.
